title: Concrete Blonde

infobox.value: * [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") * [gothic rock](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock") * [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") * [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") (early)

* [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano") * [James Mankey](/wiki/James_Mankey "James Mankey") * Gabriel Ramirez * [Harry Rushakoff](/wiki/Harry_Rushakoff "Harry Rushakoff") * [Paul Thompson](/wiki/Paul_Thompson_(musician) "Paul Thompson (musician)") * Al Bloch

was an American [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band from [Hollywood, California](/wiki/Hollywood,_California "Hollywood, California"). They were active from 1982 to 1994 and reunited twice: first from 2001 to 2004, then from 2010 to 2012. They were best known for their album *[Bloodletting](/wiki/Bloodletting_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Bloodletting (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1990), its top-20 single "[Joey](/wiki/Joey_(Concrete_Blonde_song) "Joey (Concrete Blonde song)")," and [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano")'s distinctive vocal style.

on the compilation album *The D.I.Y. Album* (1982).[[1]](#cite_note-1) The track was co-produced by James and his brother [Earle Mankey](/wiki/Earle_Mankey "Earle Mankey"), who also programmed the drums.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Joined by drummer Michael Murphy, they became **Dream 6** and released an [eponymous extended play](/wiki/Dream_6_(EP) "Dream 6 (EP)") on the independent label Happy Hermit in 1983 (released in France in 1985 by Madrigal).[[3]](#cite_note-3) When they signed with [I.R.S. Records](/wiki/I.R.S._Records "I.R.S. Records") in 1986, their labelmate [Michael Stipe](/wiki/Michael_Stipe "Michael Stipe") suggested the name Concrete Blonde,[[4]](#cite_note-4) describing the contrast between their hard rock music and introspective lyrics. They were joined by drummer [Harry Rushakoff](/wiki/Harry_Rushakoff "Harry Rushakoff") on their eponymous debut album.[[5]](#cite_note-St._Petersburg-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American rock band

This article is about the band. For their debut album, see [Concrete Blonde (album)](/wiki/Concrete_Blonde_(album) "Concrete Blonde (album)"). For the Michael Connelly novel, see [The Concrete Blonde](/wiki/The_Concrete_Blonde "The Concrete Blonde").

| Concrete Blonde | |
| --- | --- |
| [Concrete Blonde, 1989 lineup (left to right): Johnette Napolitano, Harry Rushakoff, Alan Bloch and Jim Mankey](/wiki/File:Concrete_Blonde.jpg "Concrete Blonde, 1989 lineup (left to right): Johnette Napolitano, Harry Rushakoff, Alan Bloch and Jim Mankey") Concrete Blonde, 1989 lineup (left to right): [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano"), Harry Rushakoff, Alan Bloch and [Jim Mankey](/wiki/Jim_Mankey "Jim Mankey") | |
| Background information | |
| Also known as | Dream 6 (1982–1986) |
| Origin | Los Angeles, California |
| Genres | * [Alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") * [gothic rock](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock") * [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") * [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") (early) |
| Years active | * 1982–1994 * 1997 (one-off reunion) * 2001–2004 * 2010–2012 |
| Label | [I.R.S.](/wiki/I.R.S._Records "I.R.S. Records") |
|  | |
| Past members | * [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano") * [James Mankey](/wiki/James_Mankey "James Mankey") * Gabriel Ramirez * [Harry Rushakoff](/wiki/Harry_Rushakoff "Harry Rushakoff") * [Paul Thompson](/wiki/Paul_Thompson_(musician) "Paul Thompson (musician)") * Al Bloch |

**Concrete Blonde** was an American [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band from [Hollywood, California](/wiki/Hollywood,_California "Hollywood, California"). They were active from 1982 to 1994 and reunited twice: first from 2001 to 2004, then from 2010 to 2012. They were best known for their album *[Bloodletting](/wiki/Bloodletting_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Bloodletting (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1990), its top-20 single "[Joey](/wiki/Joey_(Concrete_Blonde_song) "Joey (Concrete Blonde song)")," and [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano")'s distinctive vocal style.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Singer-songwriter and bassist [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano") first formed a group with former [Sparks](/wiki/Sparks_(band) "Sparks (band)") bassist [James Mankey](/wiki/James_Mankey "James Mankey") on guitar in Los Angeles in 1982. Their first recording was the song "Heart Attack," released under the band name **Dreamers** on the compilation album *The D.I.Y. Album* (1982).[[1]](#cite_note-1) The track was co-produced by James and his brother [Earle Mankey](/wiki/Earle_Mankey "Earle Mankey"), who also programmed the drums.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Joined by drummer Michael Murphy, they became **Dream 6** and released an [eponymous extended play](/wiki/Dream_6_(EP) "Dream 6 (EP)") on the independent label Happy Hermit in 1983 (released in France in 1985 by Madrigal).[[3]](#cite_note-3) When they signed with [I.R.S. Records](/wiki/I.R.S._Records "I.R.S. Records") in 1986, their labelmate [Michael Stipe](/wiki/Michael_Stipe "Michael Stipe") suggested the name Concrete Blonde,[[4]](#cite_note-4) describing the contrast between their hard rock music and introspective lyrics. They were joined by drummer [Harry Rushakoff](/wiki/Harry_Rushakoff "Harry Rushakoff") on their eponymous debut album.[[5]](#cite_note-St._Petersburg-5)

Their first release was *[Concrete Blonde](/wiki/Concrete_Blonde_(album) "Concrete Blonde (album)")* (1986), which included their debut single "[Still in Hollywood](/wiki/Still_in_Hollywood_(song) "Still in Hollywood (song)")." They added a full-time bass guitarist, Alan Bloch, for their album *[Free](/wiki/Free_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Free (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1989), allowing Napolitano to focus on her singing without the burden of playing bass simultaneously. This album included the college radio hit "[God Is a Bullet](/wiki/God_Is_a_Bullet_(song) "God Is a Bullet (song)")."[[6]](#cite_note-allmusic-6)

Their third album, *[Bloodletting](/wiki/Bloodletting_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Bloodletting (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1990), became their most commercially successful, reaching No. 4 in Canada, No. 8 in Australia, and No. 49 in the United States. It was certified gold in both the United States and Australia. [Roxy Music](/wiki/Roxy_Music "Roxy Music") drummer [Paul Thompson](/wiki/Paul_Thompson_(musician) "Paul Thompson (musician)") replaced Rushakoff on *Bloodletting* while Rushakoff was in treatment for drug addiction.[[5]](#cite_note-St._Petersburg-5) Napolitano also reassumed bass duties for the recording, and Bloch does not appear on the album or any subsequent releases. The album was certified gold by the RIAA[[7]](#cite_note-7) and included their highest-charting single, "[Joey](/wiki/Joey_(Concrete_Blonde_song) "Joey (Concrete Blonde song)")," which spent 21 weeks on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* Top 100 chart, peaking at No. 19 in the United States and No. 2 in Australia.[[8]](#cite_note-billboard.com-8)

*[Walking in London](/wiki/Walking_in_London_(album) "Walking in London (album)")* (1992) saw the return of original drummer Rushakoff due to Thompson's immigration issues, while its successor, *[Mexican Moon](/wiki/Mexican_Moon "Mexican Moon")* (1993), featured the *Bloodletting* lineup with Thompson back on drums. Neither album achieved the commercial success of *Bloodletting,* and Napolitano disbanded the group in 1994.[[9]](#cite_note-Billboard-9)

The band reunited in 1997, with Napolitano and Mankey collaborating with the band [Los Illegals](/wiki/Los_Illegals "Los Illegals") on the album *[Concrete Blonde y Los Illegals](/wiki/Concrete_Blonde_y_Los_Illegals "Concrete Blonde y Los Illegals")*. The vocals were primarily in Spanish. During live performances, the band altered the refrain of "Still in Hollywood" to "Still in the Barrio" and included covers of [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin")'s "Immigrant Song" and [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix")'s "Little Wing".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The band reunited once more in 2001 and released the album *[Group Therapy](/wiki/Group_Therapy_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Group Therapy (Concrete Blonde album)")* (2002). The album was recorded in 10 days and featured Rushakoff again on drums. However, Rushakoff was eventually dismissed from the band for repeatedly failing to appear at scheduled performances. Initially replaced on tour by lighting technician Mike Devitt, he was later replaced permanently by Gabriel Ramirez.[[5]](#cite_note-St._Petersburg-5) The album *[Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_(album) "Mojave (album)")* followed in 2004.

## Retirement and post-retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Retirement and post-retirement")]

On June 5, 2006, Napolitano announced that the band had officially retired. A message on the Concrete Blonde website read: "Thanks to everyone who heard and believed in the music. Music lives on. Keep listening, keep believing, keep dreaming. Like a ripple, the music moves and travels and finds you. Drive to the music, make love to the music, cry to the music. That's why we made it. Long after we're gone, the music will still be there. Thanks to everyone who helped us bring the music to you & thanks to every face and every heart in every audience all over the world."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

On July 13, 2010, [Shout! Factory](/wiki/Shout!_Factory "Shout! Factory") released a remastered 20th-anniversary edition of *Bloodletting*.[[6]](#cite_note-allmusic-6) It featured six bonus tracks: "I Want You," "Little Wing," the French extended version of "Bloodletting (The Vampire Song)," and live versions of "Roses Grow," "The Sky Is a Poisonous Garden," and "Tomorrow, Wendy."[[12]](#cite_note-12) An error in the packaging of the re-release included early non-album period photos featuring original drummer Harry Rushakoff, who had been replaced the night before the first studio session for *Bloodletting* by Roxy Music drummer Paul Thompson. The band supported the reissue with the "20 Years of Bloodletting: The Vampires Rise" tour, which continued through the remainder of the year.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2012, the band released the single "Rosalie," with the B-side "I Know the Ghost." In December of that year, they embarked on a brief tour of nine cities, primarily along the East Coast of the United States.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Artistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Artistry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/X1979LA.jpg/250px-X1979LA.jpg)](/wiki/File:X1979LA.jpg)

[X](/wiki/X_(American_band) "X (American band)"), another band who emerged from the Los Angeles post-punk scene

Concrete Blonde emerged from the [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") club circuit that produced bands like [X](/wiki/X_(American_band) "X (American band)"), [Wall of Voodoo](/wiki/Wall_of_Voodoo "Wall of Voodoo"), and [the Go-Go's](/wiki/The_Go-Go%27s "The Go-Go's"). This is evident on their releases as Dream 6, and their self-titled debut album. Johnette Napolitano's songwriting and singing draws influence from [roots music](/wiki/American_country_music "American country music"), as well as Catholic and Mexican imagery, with topics in her music including sex and death. A common theme on *Concrete Blonde* is her distaste of [Hollywood, Los Angeles](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), her city of residence.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) She was compared to [Patti Smith](/wiki/Patti_Smith "Patti Smith") upon the album's release, but did not know who she was at the time. Instead, she cited [Donovan](/wiki/Donovan "Donovan") and [Linda Ronstadt](/wiki/Linda_Ronstadt "Linda Ronstadt") as influences, and has gone on record for saying she likes to be sexy, but does not see herself as a sex symbol.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As the 1980s progressed, Concrete Blonde transitioned to [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"),[[18]](#cite_note-allmusic2-18) [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock")[[19]](#cite_note-19) and [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock").[[20]](#cite_note-20) The band's production value also increased, while *Free*, their second album, saw minor success due to the single "God Is a Bullet", an alternative song with elements of hard rock and punk.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Around this time, Napolitano had been spending a lot of time in [New Orleans, Louisiana](/wiki/New_Orleans,_Louisiana "New Orleans, Louisiana") and reading *[The Vampire Chronicles](/wiki/The_Vampire_Chronicles "The Vampire Chronicles")* series of [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction") [vampire](/wiki/Vampire_literature "Vampire literature") novels by American writer [Anne Rice](/wiki/Anne_Rice "Anne Rice").[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22) As a result, they shifted to [gothic rock](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock") during the 1990s, with Jim Allen of uDiscoverMusic coining the term "alt-goth" to describe the band and comparing them to [Stevie Nicks](/wiki/Stevie_Nicks "Stevie Nicks").[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)

On top of the [gothic](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock")-influenced [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), the band's breakthrough album, *Bloodletting*, draws inspiration from [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") on the title track, and [arena rock](/wiki/Arena_rock "Arena rock") on the hit single, "Joey".[[24]](#cite_note-:4-24) Psychological horror is used throughout the album, such as "The Beast", in which the monster that breaks the narrator's heart is a metaphor for a failed relationship.[[24]](#cite_note-:4-24) They disbanded in 1994. Three years later, they had a one-off reunion, collaborating with [Chicano punk](/wiki/Chicano_punk "Chicano punk") band [Los Illegals](/wiki/Los_Illegals "Los Illegals") on *Concrete Blonde y Los Illegals*, which combined hard rock and [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music") with mostly Spanish lyrics.[[25]](#cite_note-allmusic3-25) Jim Allen of uDiscoverMusic summarized the band's musical style as "a sweetly sulfurous blend of goth, punk, and alternative rock."[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)

## Members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Members")]

* [Johnette Napolitano](/wiki/Johnette_Napolitano "Johnette Napolitano") – bass, vocals (1982–1994, 2001–2006, 2012)
* [James Mankey](/wiki/James_Mankey "James Mankey") – guitar (1982–1994, 2001–2006, 2012)
* [Harry Rushakoff](/wiki/Harry_Rushakoff "Harry Rushakoff") – drums (1985–1989, 1992, 2001–2002)
* Al Bloch – bass (1988–1989)
* [Paul Thompson](/wiki/Paul_Thompson_(musician) "Paul Thompson (musician)") – drums (1989–1991, 1993–1994)
* Mike Devitt – drums (2002)
* Gabriel Ramirez – drums (2003–2006, 2012)

### Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Timeline")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/snsevwb2wryansycvpcuv88ad3d3rkq.png)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Discography")]

### Studio albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Studio albums")]

* *[Concrete Blonde](/wiki/Concrete_Blonde_(album) "Concrete Blonde (album)")* (1986)
* *[Free](/wiki/Free_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Free (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1989)
* *[Bloodletting](/wiki/Bloodletting_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Bloodletting (Concrete Blonde album)")* (1990)
* *[Walking in London](/wiki/Walking_in_London_(album) "Walking in London (album)")* (1992)
* *[Mexican Moon](/wiki/Mexican_Moon "Mexican Moon")* (1993)
* *[Concrete Blonde y Los Illegals](/wiki/Concrete_Blonde_y_Los_Illegals "Concrete Blonde y Los Illegals")* (1997) – collaboration with a Los Angeles–based [Chicano punk](/wiki/Chicano_punk "Chicano punk") band [Los Illegals](/wiki/Los_Illegals "Los Illegals")
* *[Group Therapy](/wiki/Group_Therapy_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "Group Therapy (Concrete Blonde album)")* (2002)
* *[Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_(album) "Mojave (album)")* (2004)

### Compilation and live albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Compilation and live albums")]

* *[Still in Hollywood](/wiki/Still_in_Hollywood "Still in Hollywood")* (1994) – compilation of live recordings, B-sides, and previously unreleased material.
* *[Recollection: The Best of Concrete Blonde](/wiki/Recollection:_The_Best_of_Concrete_Blonde "Recollection: The Best of Concrete Blonde")* (1996) – compilation of 17 tracks from first five albums, plus live cover version of [Mercedes Benz](/wiki/Mercedes_Benz_(song) "Mercedes Benz (song)").
* *Classic Masters* (2002) – 24-bit remastering of 12 tracks from first five albums.
* *[Live in Brazil 2002](/wiki/Live_in_Brazil_2002 "Live in Brazil 2002")* (2003) – double live album.
* *[The Essential](/wiki/The_Essential_(Concrete_Blonde_album) "The Essential (Concrete Blonde album)")* (2005) – includes 14 remastered tracks from first four albums, and a different version of the song "Sun".

### Non-album tracks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Non-album tracks")]

See also: [Still in Hollywood](/wiki/Still_in_Hollywood "Still in Hollywood")

* I Want You – B-side on *[Joey](/wiki/Joey_(Concrete_Blonde_song) "Joey (Concrete Blonde song)")* [single] (1990); [*Point Break* soundtrack](/wiki/Point_Break#Soundtrack "Point Break") (1991) – included on 20th anniversary edition of "Bloodletting" album.
* Crystal Blue Persuasion – *In Defense of Animals* (1993), compilation album.
* [Mercedes Benz (Live)](/wiki/Mercedes_Benz_(song) "Mercedes Benz (song)") (Janis Joplin) – included on *[Recollection: The Best of Concrete Blonde](/wiki/Recollection:_The_Best_of_Concrete_Blonde "Recollection: The Best of Concrete Blonde")* (1996)
* Endless Sleep (Dolores Nance/[Jody Reynolds](/wiki/Jody_Reynolds "Jody Reynolds")) – *[Fast Track to Nowhere](/wiki/Fast_Track_to_Nowhere "Fast Track to Nowhere")* (1994), soundtrack album to the series *[Rebel Highway](/wiki/Rebel_Highway "Rebel Highway")*
* The God in You (MantraMix) – *Roxy CD single* (2002)
* Joey (live, acoustic) – *Live from the CD101 Big Room (Vol. 1)* (2003)
* Sun (alternate version) – included on *The Essential Concrete Blonde* (2005)
* Rosalie / I Know The Ghost (J. Napolitano) – *Rosalie* [single], 2011[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concrete_Blonde&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Singles")]
